coredata.dc:title: Substituent derivatives of benzothiazole-based fluorescence probes for hydrazine with conspicuous luminescence properties: A theoretical study
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Yunjian
Shang, Changjiao
Zheng, Zefei
Sun, Chaofan
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, four probe molecules for detecting hydrazine have been designed based on the 2-(4-Acetoxy-3-benzothiazole-2-yl-phenyl)-4-methyl-thiazole- 5-carboxylic acid ethyl ester (HP1) to investigate the influence of the amino and cyano groups on the excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) behavior and photophysical properties. The changes in hydrogen bond strength indicate that the intramolecular hydrogen bond of all probe products is enhanced upon photoexcitation. Frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) and natural bond orbital (NBO) reveal the driving force of ESIPT. In addition, the potential energy curves and transition state theory explain the reason for the single fluorescence phenomenon in the experiment. The simulated absorption and fluorescence spectra of HP1 and its product (HPP1) are completely consistent with the experimental results, which also verify the viewpoint. Meanwhile the cyano derivative HPP4 exhibits a larger Stokes-shift (201 nm) than that of HPP1 (145 nm) and has the same low energy barrier as HPP1. These excellent properties allow HPP4 to be a fluorescent probe with superior performance than the original molecule. In conclusion, this work can provide a theoretical basis for the design and synthesis of more sensitive fluorescent probes for the detection of hydrazine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrazine probe
ESIPT
Density functional theory
Substitution effect
Large Stokes-shift

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Molecular structures of five probes for hydrazine and their products at S1 state. (White: H; Gray: C; Red: O; Blue: N; Yellow: S.).
Calculated infrared spectra of –OH group stretching vibration for HPP1 and its derivatives at the S0 and S1 states.
The RDG scatter plots of HPP1 and its derivatives (enol form).
The simulated absorption and fluorescence spectra for five probes and their products in DMSO solvent.
The natural transition orbits of five compounds.
Potential energy curves of the HPP1 and its derivatives at S0 and S1 states.
Transition state energy of HPP1 and its derivatives.
The molecular structures of five probe molecules and their products.
Bond lengths (Å) and bond angles (°) of HPP1 and its derivatives.
The transition properties of five probes for hydrazine and their products in DMSO solvent using TD-DFT/B3PW91/6-31G(d,p) method.
NBO charge distribution (a.u.) of the O1 and N3 atoms for five probes for hydrazine and their products.

head.ce:title: Substituent derivatives of benzothiazole-based fluorescence probes for hydrazine with conspicuous luminescence properties: A theoretical study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Aulin College, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Aulin College, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, four probe molecules for detecting hydrazine have been designed based on the 2-(4-Acetoxy-3-benzothiazole-2-yl-phenyl)-4-methyl-thiazole- 5-carboxylic acid ethyl ester (HP1) to investigate the influence of the amino and cyano groups on the excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) behavior and photophysical properties. The changes in hydrogen bond strength indicate that the intramolecular hydrogen bond of all probe products is enhanced upon photoexcitation. Frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) and natural bond orbital (NBO) reveal the driving force of ESIPT. In addition, the potential energy curves and transition state theory explain the reason for the single fluorescence phenomenon in the experiment. The simulated absorption and fluorescence spectra of HP1 and its product (HPP1) are completely consistent with the experimental results, which also verify the viewpoint. Meanwhile the cyano derivative HPP4 exhibits a larger Stokes-shift (201 nm) than that of HPP1 (145 nm) and has the same low energy barrier as HPP1. These excellent properties allow HPP4 to be a fluorescent probe with superior performance than the original molecule. In conclusion, this work can provide a theoretical basis for the design and synthesis of more sensitive fluorescent probes for the detection of hydrazine.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrazine is a colorless oily liquid with corrosive, strong reducing and improved detonable properties, which makes it useful for industrial production, pharmaceuticals, jet engines fuel, and so on. At the same time, hydrazine is a highly toxic substance that is soluble in water, alcohol and other polar solvents, which is also a suspected carcinogen due to its damaging effects on many human organs [1-5]. Beyond that, the widespread use of hydrazine inevitably causes environmental pollution. Therefore, it is essential to detect whether hydrazine is present in water and cells. Many traditional methods have been studied for hydrazine detection, but these methods have different disadvantages such as low sensitivity, expensive, time-consuming and complex operation [6-9]. However, with the development of technology, the emergence of the fluorescence probe technique has solved these problems [10-12].

In recent decades, the fluorescence probe technique for detecting various substances in cells has attracted the attention of many researchers because of its high sensitivity, accuracy and real-time monitoring. To date, there are many mechanisms used to construct fluorescence probes, such as intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [13], changing the π-conjugation system (CCS) [14], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [15], excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) [16-19], aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [20], photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [21-24]. Among them, the fluorescent probes based on the ESIPT mechanism have been widely studied. The ESIPT process refers to the transfer of proton from hydroxyl or amino group to neighboring heteroatom within the excited-state molecule and then forming an isomeric form. The process can produce large Stokes-shifts and enhance the photochemical stability of molecules, which are well suitable for fluorescent probes.

More and more fluorescent probes based on the ESIPT behavior have been designed to detect hydrazine. For example, a new fluorescence probe was synthesized based on 2-(2′-hydroxyphenyl) benzothiazole (HBT) by introducing 4-bromobutyric acid as a protecting group, which eliminated proton and thus inhibited the ESIPT process. The probe can sensitively and selectively detect hydrazine below the detection limit [4]. Similarly, another novel probe with a lower detection limit was obtained by introducing carbonate as protecting group on the HBT molecule [9]. Moreover, two fluorescent chemo dosimeters based on the phenolic ester derivative of benzothiazoles were investigated to detect hydrazine in the environment and living cells [25]. These mentioned studies have experimentally validated its specificity for hydrazine detection and can be used in living cells. However, the photophysical properties of probe molecules need to be further explored.

In this work, we selected the hydrazine probe 2-(4-Acetoxy-3-benzothiazole-2-yl -phenyl)-4-methyl-thiazole-5-carboxylic acid ethyl ester (named HP1) with the lower detection limit for our theoretical study [25]. It is clear that the detection mechanism of HP1 is to remove the acetic acid group on the probe after reacting with hydrazine, thus recovering the –OH group and switching on the ESIPT process. Therefore, we mainly analyzed the driving force and internal mechanism of the ESIPT process for product HPP1. In addition, to obtain the probe molecules with more prominent luminescence properties, the cyano (–CN) and amino (–NH2) groups were separately introduced at different positions to modulate the ESIPT behavior of the HPP1 molecule (Scheme 1 ). Here, we will discuss the effects of the type and position for the substituent group on the probe molecules from different perspectives (including geometry parameters, infrared analysis, frontier molecular orbitals, potential energy curves, and so on). The calculated absorption-fluorescence spectra will be used for comparison with experiments and to predict the luminescence phenomenon of the designed probes.

In this work, the density functional theory (DFT) [26,27] and time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) [28-30] methods with B3LYP functional [31-33] and 6-31G(d,p) basis set [34-36] were employed to optimize the S0 and S1 structures of these probe molecules and their products without any restriction. To simulate the experimental environment more accurately, the theoretical calculations were conducted in the dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solvent with the integral equation formalism polarization continuum model (IEFPCM) [37,38]. The structural parameters, infrared vibrations (IR) and reduced density gradient (RDG) scatter plots [39-41] were analyzed to determine the intensity changes of hydrogen bond upon photoexcitation. The natural transition orbit (NTO) and frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) were mapped by using Multiwfn [42] and VMD program [43], respectively. In addition, the potential energy curves of HPP1 and its derivatives were scanned by gradually increasing the O1-H2 distance in steps of 0.1 Å. All of the quantum calculations were performed employing Gaussian 16 software [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometric structure parameter and infrared vibrational analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The S0- and S1-state structures of five probes and their products (called HP and HPP), including normal (enol) and photo-isomeric (keto) forms, were optimized at the B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level. The structures of these studied molecules are shown in Fig. 1 and the critical atoms of hydrogen bonds are labeled in HPP1. The hydrogen bond parameters of these probes and their derivatives are recorded in Table 1 . Most notably, the S0-state structures optimization of HPP2 and HPP4 were ended to enol form. That is, the keto forms were returned to enol in a no-barrier process.

As listed in Table 1, the distance of the intramolecular hydrogen bond (IHB) changed in varying degrees after being excited to the S1 state. For the enol forms of HPP1, HPP3, HPP4 and HPP5, the O1-H2 bond length is separately extended from 0.999 Å, 1.000 Å, 0.995 Å, and 0.996 Å in the S0 state to 1.059 Å, 1.001 Å, 1.040 Å and 1.049 Å in the S1 state, increasing by 0.06 Å, 0.001 Å, 0.045 Å, and 0.053 Å. The distance of IHB H2…N3 is shorted from 1.701 Å, 1.691 Å, 1.722 Å, and 1.716 Å in the S0 state to 1.503 Å, 1.694 Å, 1.557 Å and 1.531 Å in the S1 state, diminishing by 0.198 Å, 0.003 Å, 0.165 Å, and 0.185 Å, respectively. At the same time, the bond angle O1-H2…N3 of IHB expands from 147.8°, 148.1°, 146.7° and 147.1° in the S0 state to 152.1°, 150.0°, 151.3° and 152.1° in the S1 state, respectively. For the initial configuration of HPP2, the bond length of O1-H2 and H2…N3, as well as the bond angle O1-H2…N3 decreased 0.001 Å, increased 0.002 Å and 2.1°, respectively. All of these indicate that the IHBs of HPP1 and its derivatives at the S1 state are enhanced, which is beneficial to the ESIPT process. The degree of change in hydrogen bonds obeys the order of HPP2 < HPP3 < HPP4 < HPP5 < HPP1. In summary, compared with HPP1, the introduction of substituent group will reduce the change of hydrogen bond strength and the degree of influence for the amino group is greater than the cyano group. Concerning the same substituent, the effect of meta position on IHB strength is relatively weaker than that of ortho position. In addition, for the keto form of HPP1, HPP3 and HPP5, the changes in structure parameters are opposite to that of the enol form, which indicates that the IHBs of keto forms are stronger in the S0 state. In other words, these keto forms at the S1 state could easily radiate back to the S0 state.

Since the trend of the stretching vibration for the proton donor can uncover the change in IHB strength [45-48], the infrared (IR) vibration spectra of HPP1 and its derivatives were simulated and presented in Fig. 2 . As shown in the figure, HPP1 and its derivatives produced different red-shift. The vibration frequency of O1 H2 bond for HPP1, HPP2, HPP3, HPP4 and HPP5 are separately shifted from 3146 cm−1, 3200 cm−1, 3112 cm−1, 3227 cm−1, 3204 cm−1 at the S0 state to 2133 cm−1, 3184 cm−1, 3082 cm−1, 2426 cm−1, 2282 cm−1 at the S1 state. The red-shift values for the five compounds are 1013 cm−1, 16 cm−1, 30 cm−1, 801 cm−1, 922 cm−1 in the order of HPP2 < HPP3 < HPP4 < HPP5 < HPP1, which also demonstrates that the IHBs are reinforced. However, compared with HPP1, the hydrogen bond strength will be weaker whether the cyano or amino group is introduced at meta and ortho positions. This result for the hydrogen bond change is consistent with the conclusion of the geometric parameters section.

The RDG analysis method was proposed by Yang and colleagues for the analysis of weak interactions that has been widely used to examine inter- and intra-molecular weak interactions [41]. The expression of RDG is as following:
R D G
r
=
1
2
3 π 2
1 / 3
| ∇ ρ ( r ) |
ρ ( r )
4 / 3

In the above equation, ∇ is the gradient operator, r and ρ(r) represents the coordinate vector and electron density, respectively. ρ(r) can only show the reaction intensity and the type of interaction needs to be reflected by the Sign(λ2). The RDG scatter plots for HPP1 and its derivatives were obtained by Multiwfn program based on the optimized structures, and drawn in Fig. 3 (enol structure) and Fig.S1 (keto structure). The position of the blue spike represents the strength of the hydrogen bond, which can give a more visual observation of the change in hydrogen bond strength. As can be seen from Fig. 3, the blue spike of HPP1 is located around −0.05 a.u. at the S0 state and about −0.085 a.u. at the S1 state. In terms of HPP2 and HPP3, the blue spike of the ground state is almost constant compared to the excited state. For HPP4 and HPP5, the blue spikes of ground and excited states correspond to −0.05 a.u., −0.05 a.u. and −0.08 a.u. < ρ < -0.07 a.u., −0.08 a.u., respectively. The change in IHBs strength means that the five probe products are easily photo-excited to the S1 state and the hydrogen bonds become stronger. The order of IHBs strength in the excited state is HPP2 < HPP3 < HPP4 < HPP5 < HPP1, which is fully agrees with the conclusions presented above. For the keto forms of five products (Fig.S1), their hydrogen bonds for HPP1, HPP3 and HPP5 at the S0 state are obviously stronger than the keto structure at the S1 state. As for the HPP2 and HPP4, their keto forms are unstable and will undergo reverse proton transfer at the ground state.

In order to predict the possible luminescence phenomenon in the experiment, the absorption-fluorescence spectra of probes and their derivatives were calculated before and after the addition of hydrazine. The transition and fluorescence properties are gathered in Table 2 and Table S2, respectively (the transition properties of the first six excited states are shown in Table S1). As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the maximum absorption peak of HP1 is located at 311 nm, as well as the fluorescence peaks of enol and keto structures for HPP1 are at 416 nm and 507 nm, respectively, which fits well with the experimental phenomenon (color change from colorless to green) upon addition of hydrazine and the fluorescence peak of keto form 507 nm is consistent with the single fluorescence peak 507 nm measured in the experiment [25]. This result testifies the reliability and accuracy of the theoretical calculation. Furthermore, the single fluorescence phenomenon will be further explained in combination with the potential energy curves. The keto form of HPP1 is dominant since its enol form only needs to cross a very small energy barrier and undergoes an ultrafast ESIPT process to become the keto form, which in turn emits fluorescence.

As presented in Fig. 4, the Stokes-shift of keto configurations for HPP1 and its derivatives are 145 nm, 117 nm, 163 nm, 201 nm and 177 nm, respectively. It is worthwhile noticing that the substitution of the electron-withdrawing group –CN can lead to longer fluorescence (546 nm and 522 nm) and larger Stokes-shift compared to HPP1. Most interestingly, the introduction of the electron-donating group –NH2 causes the fluorescence quenching for the probe molecules (HP2 and HP3), and the probe products (HPP2 and HPP3) emit stronger fluorescence than HPP1. On balance, the luminescence phenomenon produced by substitution in the ortho position is more pronounced than that in the meta position. Compared with the original probe, the introduction of the amino group quenched the fluorescence of the probe and enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the keto form for the probe product, while the cyano group brought about a larger Stokes-shift upon photo-excitation. These properties have great application value for fluorescent probes or other fields related to luminescence.

It is well known that the properties of luminescence are closely related to the electronic transition. Therefore, the electron distribution is conducive to understand the internal luminescence mechanism for these probes and their products. Based on the structure optimization, the FMOs and natural bond orbital (NBO) charge population were obtained to visualize the distribution of the electron cloud and the NBO charge for the hydrogen bond donor–acceptor atoms (O1 and N3), respectively [49-52]. As shown in Table S2, it can be found that the dominant transition orbitals for probe molecules and their products correspond to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), so these orbitals of five probe products are plotted in Fig.S2 and Fig.S3.

According to the distribution of the electron cloud, it is clear that HP2 and HP3 as well as their products undergo a distinct intramolecular charge transition process upon photo-excitation. Such obvious charge transfer results in the quenching of the fluorescence of HP2 and HP3, as well as the change of the fluorescence intensity for their respective products. In contrast, the other probe molecules and their products are coupled by local excitation and intramolecular charge transition. In addition, the energy gaps of each molecule were also marked in Fig.S3, and the reduction of the gaps were closely related to the photophysical properties. It is worth mentioning that the HPP4 and HPP5 have the same trend: the electron density on the O1 and N3 atoms is separately lessened and increased after the transition from HOMO to LUMO, which indicates that the transition could provide the driving force for the ESIPT process. However, for HPP2 and HPP3, the electron distribution of the N3 atom is reduced in LUMO, and their ESIPT process is somewhat challenging compared to HPP1.

To study the charge change during the ESIPT process more intuitively, the NBO charge population of the hydrogen bond donor–acceptor atoms (O1 and N3) has been calculated and gathered in Table 3 . For HP1 and its derivatives, the charges of O1 and N3 atoms at the S0 and S1 states hardly change due to the introduction of the protective group. By comparing the probe molecules and their respective products, it can be found that removing the protecting group will result in a significant enhancement of the charges on the O1 and N3 atoms. Taking HPP1 as an example, the calculated NBO charge of O1 atom and N3 atom separately decreased and increased from −0.698 a.u. and −0.523 a.u. in the S0 state to −0.636 a.u. and −0.568 a.u. in the S1 state, which demonstrates that the attraction of O1 atom to proton is weakened while that of N3 atom is enhanced. The probe products HPP4 and HPP5 have the same trend. This means that these changes are beneficial to the ESIPT process. Nevertheless, the charge of O1 atom for HPP2 and HPP3 increased from −0.699 a.u. and −0.700 a.u. at the S0 state to −0.714 a.u. at the S1 state, respectively. Similarly, the charge of N3 atom enlarges from −0.527 a.u. to −0.623 a.u. and −0.606 a.u.. The changes in the charge of O1 and N3 atoms indicates that the promotion of the ESIPT process is weaker than that of HPP1, and the results are consistent with the conclusion reached above. In a few words, introducing the amino group at ortho (R1) and meta (R2) positions will inhibit the ESIPT process for the original product HPP1, but the introduction of the cyano group can facilitate the ESIPT reaction.

Furthermore, we calculated the corresponding FMO contributions of HPP1 and its derivatives from S0 → S1 are 91.01%, 92.14%, 77.74%, 92.89% and 71.41%, respectively. Obvious this is not sufficient, thus the NTO analysis was further used to analyze the orbital transition properties of S0 → S1 and the orbital contribution. As shown in Fig. 5 , the NTO pair contributions of these studied molecules are all above 97.82%, which is adequate to be considered as a dominant effect. Therefore, in terms of the features of these NTO pairs, we can judge that the main characteristics of the transition from S0 to S1 are all π → π*.

In order to investigate whether these five probe products undergo the ESIPT behavior, their potential energy curves were scanned by extending the distance of the O1-H2 bond. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 6 that the energy barrier of the five probe products (HPP1, HPP2, HPP3, HPP4, HPP5) in the S0 state are 6.91 kcal/mol, 10.47 kcal/mol, 6.28 kcal/mol, 11.04 kcal/mol, and 7.93 kcal/mol, which are higher than the barriers in the S1 state (0.06 kcal/mol, 5.78 kcal/mol, 3.75 kcal/mol, 0.63 kcal/mol and 0.38 kcal/mol). The phenomenon suggests that these ESIPT reactions are susceptible to happen upon photo-excitation. By comparing the forward and reverse barriers of HPP1 and its derivatives, the following conclusions can be drawn: taking HPP1 as an example, the forward energy barrier of its S1 state is 0.06 kcal/mol, which means that it will form the isomeric structure through undergoing an ultra-fast ESIPT process. Then the excited-state isomer emits fluorescence back to the ground state, and it only needs to cross the reverse energy barrier of 0.23 kcal/mol to return to the normal form. In addition, the potential energy curves prove that the enol and keto configurations are more stable separately in the S0 and S1 states, which is completely consistent with the previous conclusions in the hydrogen bond analysis section. In particular, HPP2 and HPP4 have no reverse barrier in the S0 state, which is why these two molecules have only enol configuration in the S0 state as described in the geometric structure section.

Furthermore, for HPP2 and HPP3, the substitution of amino group at the ortho and meta positions on the benzothiazole increases the S1-state energy barrier to 5.78 kcal/mol and 3.75 kcal/mol, respectively, which elucidates that their ESIPT reactions would be slower than HPP1. However, the energy barrier of the cyano derivatives is almost the same as that of HPP1. The forward energy barriers of HPP4 and HPP5 in the S1 state are 0.63 kcal/mol and 0.38 kcal/mol, respectively. Therefore, we infer that the ESIPT process of HPP1 and its –CN derivative will take place at the same scale. Interestingly, introducing the –NH2 and –CN group at the ortho site will eliminate the reverse energy barrier of the S0 state, allowing the keto form to easily change back to the enol form.

The transition state of HPP1 and its derivatives were scanned to obtain more accurate energy barrier, as shown in Fig. 7 . Using the energy of enol form for each molecule as the initial value, the energy changes of the corresponding transition state and the keto form were compared. It can be found that the order of the forward and reverse energy barrier is consistent with the potential energy curve: HPP1 < HPP5 < HPP4 < HPP3 < HPP2. The results indicate that the introduction of –NH2 and –CN groups inhibited the ESIPT reaction of HPP1 to varying degrees. While for the same group, the substitution effect at meta position is weaker.

In this work, the ESIPT process of the probe product HPP1 and its derivatives was investigated by DFT and TD-DFT methods, and the specific source of the single fluorescence in the experiment was analyzed. By analyzing the geometrical structure parameters, IR vibrations and RDG scatter plots, it is found that the hydrogen bonds of five molecules are all enhanced at the S1 state and the degrees of change for them follow the order HPP2 < HPP3 < HPP4 < HPP5 < HPP1. The simulated absorption and fluorescence spectra of HPP1 were compared with the experimental values to verify the accuracy and feasibility of the theoretical work. The result of FMOs and NBO analysis uncovers that intramolecular charge transfer is an intrinsic driver of ESIPT process. The potential energy curves and transition state show that HPP4 and HPP5, like HPP1, can undergo an ultra-fast ESIPT process to the keto form and thus emit fluorescence. In general, the largest Stokes shift of HPP4, the fluorescence quenching of HP3 and the stronger fluorescence of HPP3 are all important properties as fluorescence probes. In addition, according the change of Stokes-shift energy barrier, we speculated that the ortho-position derivatives HPP2 and HPP4 have more notable effect than that of meta position. These discoveries that modulate ESIPT behavior by introducing different types of moieties, thereby affecting its photophysical properties, are expected to provide a reference for the future design and synthesis of novel probes with better detection effects.

Yunjian Cao: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Changjiao Shang: Investigation, Resources. Zefei Zheng: Investigation, Resources. Chaofan Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Software, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572020BC03).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121449.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
